title: Psalms 90
subtitle: A Prayer of Moses: Teach Us to Number Our Days

sections.header: Verses 1’11 — “A Thousand Years Are Like a Day”

heb.paragraph: "Lord, you have been our dwelling place (māʿōn) throughout all generations" (v.1). BOOK IV OPENS (Psalms 90–106). The only psalm attributed to Moses — the oldest in the Psalter. God is eternal; humans are dust returned (v.3). "A thousand years in your sight are like a day that has just gone by" (v.4). Peter quotes this in 2 Peter 3:8. We are swept away like a dream, like grass that springs up in the morning and withers by evening (vv.5–6). "Who knows the power of your anger?" (v.11). The brevity of life against the infinity of God.

notes.note: "Lord, you have been our dwelling place throughout all generations. Before the mountains were born, you are God." Eternal refuge: God precedes creation.
"You turn people back to dust. A thousand years in your sight are like a day." The scale differential: God’s time and human time are incommensurable. Peter quotes v.4 (2 Pet 3:8).
"People are like the new grass of the morning — in the morning it springs up new, but by evening it is dry and withered." The grass metaphor: humanity’s entire lifespan is one day of vegetation.
"Our days may come to seventy years, or eighty, if our strength endures; yet the best of them are but trouble and sorrow." The honest assessment of a long life: mostly difficulty.

refs.note: A thousand years like a day directly quotes and develops this psalm's time perspective.
Returning to dust echoes the psalm's 'you turn people back to dust.'
Life as mist appearing briefly develops the psalm's imagery of transient human existence.

hist.context: Psalm 90 opens Book IV (Pss 90-106) and is uniquely attributed to Moses, making it potentially the oldest psalm. It addresses human mortality against divine eternity. 'A thousand years in your sight are like a day' (v.4) establishes the radical disproportion between divine and human time scales.

sections.header: Verses 12–17 — “Teach Us to Number Our Days”

heb.paragraph: "Teach us to number our days, that we may gain a heart of wisdom (ḥāk̅mâ)" (v.12). The psalm’s most quoted verse and its theological heart. Numbering days is not counting them but weighing them. Awareness of mortality produces wisdom. Moses asks: satisfy us in the morning with ḥesed (v.14), make us glad for as many days as we have been afflicted (v.15). "May the favour of the Lord our God rest on us; establish the work of our hands for us" (v.17).

notes.note: "Teach us to number our days, that we may gain a heart of wisdom." Mortality-awareness as the path to wisdom. Ecclesiastes and Psalm 90 share this conviction: knowing you will die teaches you how to live.
"Satisfy us in the morning with your unfailing love, that we may sing for joy and be glad all our days." The request: let ḥesed meet us at dawn. If the morning is satisfied, the day is redeemed.
"May the favour of the Lord our God rest on us; establish the work of our hands." The prayer for significance: let our brief work have lasting impact. Establish what our mortality would erase.

refs.note: Making the most of every opportunity because days are evil develops the psalm's wisdom.
Redeeming the time echoes the psalm's 'teach us to number our days.'
Remembering the Creator before the difficult days come parallels the psalm's mortality awareness.

hist.context: The famous prayer 'teach us to number our days, that we may gain a heart of wisdom' (v.12) asks for mortality-awareness that produces wise living. The closing petitions for God's favor and established work (vv.13-17) seek meaning within brief lifespans. If Mosaic, the wilderness generation's shortened lives provide context.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: Before the mountains were born, you are God…

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVBefore the mountains were born or you brought forth the whole world, from everlasting to everlasting you are God.ESVBefore the mountains were brought forth, or ever you had formed the earth and the world, from everlasting to everlasting you are God.NIVAll our days pass away under your wrath; we finish our years with a moan.ESVFor all our days pass away under your wrath; we bring our years to an end like a sigh.

rec.text: ""Lord, you have been our dwelling place throughout all generations. Before the mountains were born, you are God." Eternal refuge: God precedes creation."

rows.label: Dust and Grass — 90:1–11
rows.text: "A thousand years are like a day." Grass: up in the morning, dry by evening. Moses’ psalm.

hebtext.note: "Lord, you have been our dwelling place (māʿōn) throughout all generations" (v.1). BOOK IV OPENS (Psalms 90–106). The only psalm attributed to Moses — the oldest in the Psalter. God is eternal; humans are dust returned (v.3). "A thousand years in your sight are like a day that has just gone by" (v.4). Peter quotes this in 2 Peter 3:8. We are swept away like a dream, like grass that springs up in the morning and withers by evening (vv.5–6). "Who knows the power of your anger?" (v.11). The brevity of life against the infinity of God.

"Teach us to number our days, that we may gain a heart of wisdom (ḥāk̅mâ)" (v.12). The psalm’s most quoted verse and its theological heart. Numbering days is not counting them but weighing them. Awareness of mortality produces wisdom. Moses asks: satisfy us in the morning with ḥesed (v.14), make us glad for as many days as we have been afflicted (v.15). "May the favour of the Lord our God rest on us; establish the work of our hands for us" (v.17).

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: God’s eternity vs human brevity. Faith: numbering days produces wisdom. Mercy: satisfy us in the morning. The oldest psalm (Moses), opening Book IV.

prayer_prompt: Moses prays for wisdom to number our days rightly. Consider asking God to give you that wisdom — to see your life's brevity and live with appropriate urgency.
